q: According to JP 3-52, what is the definition of airspace control?
options: The authority to direct ground fires in support of air operations
The assignment of radio frequencies to aircraft operating in a joint operations area
The destruction of enemy aircraft within a defined area of operations
The exercise of delegated authority over designated airspace and users through control procedures and coordination measures to maximize operational effectiveness
explain: Airspace control is defined as the exercise of delegated authority over designated airspace and users through control procedures and coordination measures to maximize operational effectiveness (p.1).

q: Which entities are listed as Marine Corps TAC C2 capable entities?
options: E-2 Hawkeye and guided missile cruisers
TAOC, DASC, and early warning and control
Brigade aviation element and air defense airspace management
AWACS, CRC, BCC, ASOC, ACIT, and ISET
explain: The Marine Corps TAC C2 capable entities are the TAOC, DASC, and early warning and control (p.2).

q: The BMAs assigned to the ASOC and DASC are normally located where relative to the fire support coordination line and coordinating altitude?
options: Within civil airspace under air traffic control
Beyond the fire support coordination line and above a coordinating altitude
Along the forward line of own troops only
Short of the fire support coordination line and below a coordinating altitude
explain: These BMAs are normally short of the fire support coordination line and below a coordinating altitude (p.3).

q: What does TACADMIN consist of?
options: Controlling air traffic for arrival and departure from an operating base
Navigation in civil airspace under civilian air traffic control
Only the coordination of air assets outside the managed JOA
All processes and procedures that occur in the TAC C2 JOA or BMA
explain: TACADMIN consists of all processes and procedures that occur in the TAC C2 JOA or BMA (p.5).

q: Which type of transmission uses the call sign of the entity being directed?
explain: Directive transmissions use the call sign of the entity being directed, e.g., HORNET 1 directing 'HORNET 2, TARGET NORTH GROUP' (p.6).

q: On what kind of NET should aircrew establish communication with TAC C2 for check-in?
explain: Aircrew should establish communication with TAC C2 on a dedicated check-in NET, which supports TACADMIN without interfering with employment (p.7).

q: How is ALPHA CHECK correlation defined, and who is responsible for solving for correlation?
options: Within 3 nm, and the aircraft checking in is responsible for solving for correlation
Within 1 nm, and the mission commander is responsible
Within 10 nm, and the final controller is responsible
Within 5 nm, and TAC C2 is responsible for solving for correlation
explain: ALPHA CHECK correlation is defined as within 3 nm, and solving for correlation rests with the aircraft checking in (p.8).

q: What does "AS FRAGGED" mean during check-in?
options: The flight is reporting a fuel or weapons emergency
The aircraft has deviations from its tasked loadout
The unit or element is performing as briefed, in accordance with the ATO or air plan
The aircraft is unable to establish positive ID
explain: AS FRAGGED means the unit or element is performing as briefed, in accordance with the air tasking order (ATO) or air plan (p.9).

q: If an ID difference exists on a link track, on which net will coordination be accomplished to verify the correct ID?
explain: If an ID difference exists on a link track, coordination will be accomplished on the track supervision net to verify the correct ID (p.10).

q: According to the note accompanying the track ID tables, who holds responsibility for dropping a track once the striking aircraft calls SPLASH?
options: The first platform to correlate the kill
explain: The first platform to correlate a kill holds the responsibility for dropping the track once the striking aircraft calls SPLASH with a BULLSEYE location and type (p.11).

q: When should fighters communicate JACKAL SOUR to controllers?
options: During check-in or during the mission when they are not receiving accurate surveillance information
When their data link fidelity check passes
explain: Fighters should communicate JACKAL SOUR to controllers during check-in or during the mission when they are not receiving accurate surveillance information (p.12).

q: According to the note on MICKEY, what is unique about an E-3 AWACS manual MICKEY?
explain: E-3 AWACS manual MICKEY is single tone only; a multiple tone MICKEY is expected from other TAC C2 agencies (p.13).

q: When should INFLTREPs be passed to TAC C2?
options: Upon checking out from a JOA, BMA, or before returning to base
explain: INFLTREPs should be passed to TAC C2 upon checking out from a JOA, BMA, or before returning to base (p.14).

q: In the fighter Inflight Report example (Table 6), what does Line 3 capture?
explain: In Table 6, Line 3 is 'Known blue attrition' (e.g., FALLEN ANGEL reports) (p.15).

q: As part of Wounded Bird, battle damage, or hung ordnance notification procedures, what should be done with the track until directed by higher headquarters?
options: Hand the track to civil air traffic control
explain: Returning-forces procedures direct controllers to FORCE TELL the track until directed by higher headquarters (p.16).

q: When TAC C2 has positive contact but uses procedural methods to deconflict, what call is transmitted when an aircraft checks in?
explain: When establishing procedural control, TAC C2 will transmit 'NEGATIVE CONTACT' when an aircraft checks in (p.17).

q: How does each aircraft, flight lead, or team lead respond during a roll call?
options: With call sign in a predetermined sequence
With an ALPHA CHECK from BULLSEYE
explain: Once a roll call is initiated, each aircraft, flight lead, or team lead will respond with call sign in a predetermined sequence (p.19).

q: After LOWDOWN is passed at a briefed time, what is it immediately followed by?
explain: LOWDOWN is passed at a briefed time (e.g., 5 minutes prior to mission execution) and is immediately followed by a roll call (p.20).

q: When disseminating tactical SAMs and MOVERS in a LOWDOWN, what determines the order of dissemination first?
options: Closest MEZ to friendly axis of attack
explain: For tactical SAMs and MOVERS, the order of dissemination begins with the closest MEZ to friendly forces (p.21).

q: Prior to switching to the alternate tactical NET, how many times will TAC C2 or MC repeat CHATTERMARK on the primary tactical NET?
explain: TAC C2 or MC will repeat CHATTERMARK three times on the primary tactical NET prior to switching to the alternate NET (p.22).

q: Once all players have checked in on the new NET after a CHATTERMARK, what does TAC C2 communicate?
explain: TAC C2 communicates "NET SWEET" on the new NET once all players have checked in (p.23).

q: Who is responsible for coordinating on-station relief before an aircraft communicates RED on the primary NET?
explain: TAC C2 is responsible to coordinate on-station relief prior to the aircraft communicating RED on the primary NET (p.24).

q: In ACC terminology, what is a GROUP used to describe?
options: Other air assets, including unknown or enemy aircraft
explain: A GROUP is the way TAC C2 and aircraft describe other air assets and is used to describe unknown or enemy aircraft (p.25).

q: Which of the following is part of the core information format used to communicate GROUPs?
options: Call sign of the controlling platform
Altitude rounded to the nearest thousands of feet
explain: The core information format includes altitude rounded to the nearest thousands of feet (p.26).

q: When a controller voices HITS, what additional information will they provide if known?
explain: Controllers will voice GEOREFs or BULLSEYE and altitude if known when voicing HITS (p.27).

q: When voicing an altitude STACK, in what order are the altitudes stated?
explain: A STACK is voiced stating the higher altitude first, then the lower altitude (p.28).

q: Specific aspects (HOT, FLANK, BEAM, DRAG) are used with which communication format?
explain: Specific aspects are used when correlating a group to a specific fighter and will be used when communicating with the BRAA format (p.29).

q: Which term do fighters use, versus controllers, to distinguish who is providing the strength assessment?
options: Fighters use STRENGTH, controllers use CONTACTs
Fighters use CONTACTs, controllers use STRENGTH
explain: Fighters will use STRENGTH and controllers will use the term CONTACTs to distinguish who is providing the strength assessment (p.30).

q: What is the defined speed range for the fill-in FAST?
explain: FAST is a target speed of 660-900 knots ground speed or 1.1-1.5 mach (p.31).

q: In cruise missile defense, which inner GROUP formation should be voiced to the maximum extent possible?
explain: In cruise missile defense (CMD), inner COVEY formations should be voiced to the maximum extent possible (p.32).

q: Unless briefed otherwise, in what format does PICTURE provide information?
explain: PICTURE is a request to provide information pertinent to the mission in a BULLSEYE format, unless briefed otherwise (p.33).

q: When a PICTURE contains four GROUPs or more, how many GROUPs are communicated?
options: Only the closest GROUP to friendly forces
Only the three highest priority GROUPs
explain: For four GROUPs or more, the call includes the total number of GROUPs but communicates only the three highest priority GROUPs (p.34).

q: For SMA, what is assumed regarding their flight profile unless deemed tactically significant?
options: CAP is assumed and track direction is not given
explain: For SMA, CAP will be assumed and track direction will not be given unless deemed to be tactically significant (p.35).

q: GROUPs will be anchored with BULLSEYE if the GROUPs are outside what distance in azimuth?
explain: GROUPs will be anchored with BULLSEYE if the GROUPs are outside 10 nm in azimuth (p.36).

q: Which of the following is one of the standard traditional labels used for a PICTURE?
explain: The standard traditional labels are SINGLE GROUP, RANGE, AZIMUTH, VIC, CHAMPAGNE, WALL, LADDER, and BOX (p.37).

q: When can OPENING or CLOSING be applied to a PICTURE?
options: When the distance between GROUPs is increasing or decreasing and may impact the fighter intercept
When fighters are in COLD operations
When a GROUP is declared HOSTILE
explain: OPENING or CLOSING can be applied when the distance between GROUPs is increasing or decreasing and may impact the fighter intercept (p.38).

q: To evaluate whether a PICTURE is WEIGHTED, how does the controller divide the azimuth?
explain: The controller divides the azimuth into thirds; if the out-of-position GROUP is outside the middle third, it is WEIGHTED (p.39).

q: WEIGHTED is accompanied by what additional descriptor?
options: An altitude block in thousands of feet
A track direction such as TRACK WEST
A range in nautical miles to BULLSEYE
A cardinal direction relative to the defined threat axis
explain: WEIGHTED is accompanied by a cardinal direction relative to the defined threat axis (p.40).

q: When is ECHELON used to amplify a traditional label?
options: When two PACKAGES are operating in separate lanes
When all GROUPs share a common HOT track direction
When only a SINGLE GROUP is detected
When GROUPs in the traditional label are not directly in AZIMUTH or RANGE with one another
explain: ECHELON is used to amplify a traditional label when GROUPs in the traditional label are not directly in AZIMUTH or RANGE with one another (p.41).

q: If all GROUPs share a common track direction other than HOT, what does the controller do?
options: Pass each GROUP as core information only
Provide track direction fill-ins on each individual GROUP
Voice the track direction amplification for the entire picture
explain: If all GROUPs share a common track direction other than hot, the controller voices the track direction amplification for the entire picture (p.44).

q: If GROUPs in a PICTURE do NOT share a common track direction, how does the controller communicate track direction?
options: Assume track direction for the whole picture
Provide track direction fill-ins on each individual GROUP
Voice a single track direction for the entire picture
explain: If GROUPs do not share a common track direction, the controller provides track direction fill-ins on each individual GROUP (p.45).

q: How are two GROUPs in AZIMUTH named?
explain: GROUPs in AZIMUTH are named using cardinal directions (p.46).

q: CHAMPAGNE or VIC is the label used under what depth condition?
options: Depth equal to or less than 5 nm
Depth greater than 5 nm or OPENING outside 5 nm
explain: CHAMPAGNE or VIC is the label used if the depth is greater than 5 nm or OPENING outside 5 nm (p.47).

q: In a WALL of five GROUPs or less, how are multiple MIDDLE GROUPs distinguished?
options: A cardinal direction is added to each so it has a unique name (e.g., NORTH MIDDLE GROUP)
They are all simply called MIDDLE GROUP
They are numbered SECOND, THIRD, FOURTH GROUP
explain: A cardinal direction is added to multiple MIDDLE GROUPs so each has a unique GROUP name, such as NORTH MIDDLE GROUP, MIDDLE GROUP, and SOUTH MIDDLE GROUP (p.48).

q: In a WALL formation with no GROUPs in the middle third, what separation is voiced?
options: Separation from the lead to the trail GROUP
Separation between the two closest outriggers
Separation from the outrigger to the middle GROUPs
explain: In a WALL formation with no GROUPs in the middle third, voice separation from the outrigger to middle GROUPs (p.49).

q: In a CHAMPAGNE, how are the two GROUPs closest to the fighters named?
options: TRAIL GROUPs with a cardinal direction descriptor
EAST GROUP and WEST GROUP only
LEAD GROUPs with a cardinal direction descriptor
explain: In a CHAMPAGNE, the two GROUPs closest to the fighters are named LEAD GROUPs with a cardinal direction descriptor, and the GROUP in trail is the TRAIL GROUP (p.50).

q: How is a VIC defined?
options: Three GROUPs with two closest in AZIMUTH and the third in RANGE
Three GROUPs with the first closest to the fighters and two GROUPs in RANGE of it, separated in AZIMUTH
Three or more GROUPs separated only in RANGE
Four GROUPs in two AZIMUTH pairs
explain: A VIC is three GROUPs with the first GROUP closest to the fighters and two GROUPs in RANGE of the first, separated in AZIMUTH (p.51).

q: In a BOX, how are the GROUPs closest to the fighters named?
options: LEAD GROUP with a cardinal direction descriptor (e.g., EAST LEAD GROUP)
TRAIL GROUP with a cardinal direction descriptor
explain: In a BOX, the GROUPs closest to the fighters are named LEAD GROUP with a cardinal direction descriptor, such as EAST LEAD GROUP and WEST LEAD GROUP (p.52).

q: Which term describes the total RANGE dimension of a LADDER?
explain: The term DEEP is used to describe the total RANGE dimension of the LADDER (p.53).

q: Besides being numbered, what else is given to the SECOND WAVE and any subsequent WAVES?
explain: The SECOND WAVE and any subsequent WAVES will also be given traditional labels (p.54).

q: Which communication terms should controllers strive to use for WAVES that are maneuvering?
explain: Controllers should strive to communicate WAVES maneuvering with REFORMING, RECOMMITTING, and PASSING communication if applicable (p.56).

q: What anchoring reference is used for an ADDITIONAL GROUP?
options: The bullseye of the LEAD GROUP
BULLSEYE in accordance with the defined anchoring priorities
explain: An ADDITIONAL GROUP is anchored using BULLSEYE in accordance with the defined anchoring priorities (p.57).

q: An undetected GROUP meets briefed THREAT criteria. What format should controllers use when issuing the call to the closest aircraft?
explain: If an undetected or unreported GROUP meets briefed THREAT criteria it is named a THREAT GROUP, and controllers should use the BRAA format when issuing a THREAT call to the closest aircraft (p.58).

q: Independent of platform or Service, Chapter V states that the air-to-air communication format applies to which aircraft?
options: Any aircraft capable of employing A/A ordnance
explain: The fighter is the example used throughout the chapter, but the information applies to any aircraft capable of employing A/A ordnance, independent of platform or Service (p.59).

q: Per Table 8, who is the priority communicator during the Targeting/Weapons Employment phase?
explain: In Table 8, during the Targeting/Weapons Employment phase the priority communicators are first the Fighters, then the Controller (p.60).

q: If only the controller retains COMMIT authority, what can the MC or TL do?
explain: If only the controller retains COMMIT authority, the controller should direct the COMMIT once criteria has been met, and the MC or TL may only recommend COMMIT (p.61).

q: How may a fighter combine targeting with shot communication?
options: By calling BANZAI after the group label
By voicing FOX (with a sort, as appropriate) after TARGETED
By adding the word ENGAGED before TARGETED
explain: Fighters may combine targeting with shot communication by voicing FOX, with a sort as appropriate, after TARGETED (p.62).

q: What information will a controller always include when responding to a DECLARE request?
options: BULLSEYE, altitude, track direction, declaration, and fill-ins
explain: Controllers will always respond to a DECLARE request with BULLSEYE, altitude, track direction, declaration, and fill-ins (p.63).

q: When does a fighter call CLEAN?
options: When the GROUP is declared FRIENDLY
When they have no sensor information on a GROUP of interest
When they have locked the GROUP
When they are merged with the GROUP
explain: Fighters will call CLEAN if they have no sensor information on a GROUP of interest (p.64).

q: Three or more CONTACTs maneuver in RANGE and AZIMUTH simultaneously while at least one remains FLANK/HOT. Which call is used?
explain: Communicate MANEUVER RANGE AND AZIMUTH if three or more CONTACTs maneuver in RANGE and AZIMUTH simultaneously while at least one CONTACT remains FLANK/HOT (p.65).

q: What does targeting range refer to?
options: The first time targeting is issued for any fighter in the element or the term TARGETED is communicated on the control net
explain: Targeting range refers to the first time targeting is issued for any fighter in the element or the term TARGETED is communicated on the control net (p.68).

q: If multiple GROUPs maneuver outside GROUP criteria, what naming should fighters and controllers use?
options: The new name (e.g., ARM or CONTACT) and the previous GROUP name (e.g., LEAD GROUP or NORTH GROUP)
A fresh sequential number for each contact
explain: If multiple GROUPs maneuver outside GROUP criteria, fighters and controllers should use the new name such as ARM or CONTACT along with the previous GROUP name such as LEAD GROUP or NORTH GROUP (p.69).

q: When does the JOINED call occur?
options: When two or more named GROUPs maneuver to meet and maintain GROUP criteria
When a GROUP maneuvers to a traditional label
When two GROUPs cross in azimuth
explain: JOINED occurs when two or more named GROUPs maneuver to meet and maintain GROUP criteria, with a new GROUP label that makes sense based on the tactical situation (p.70).

q: In what format do controllers respond to a BOGEY DOPE or BRAA request?
explain: Controllers will respond to a BOGEY DOPE/BRAA request using the BRAA format and add appropriate fill-ins (p.71).

q: If a controller cannot correlate any GROUP along the aircraft's SPIKE bearing, how should they respond?
options: With CLEAN and the bearing of the SPIKE
explain: If a controller cannot correlate any GROUP along the aircraft's SPIKE bearing or cardinal direction, they will respond with CLEAN and the bearing of the SPIKE (p.72).

q: What does the SEPARATION call provide?
options: The altitude stack of a GROUP
The aspect of the closest GROUP
The bearing from ownship to a GROUP
The distance in nm between two GROUPs or WAVEs
explain: SEPARATION is a fighter request or C2 enhancing call to provide the distance in nm between two GROUPs or WAVEs, used when a fighter is ANCHORED (p.73).

q: What is the default THREAT criteria controllers should use, independent of aspect?
explain: Controllers should use 35 nm, independent of aspect, as a default THREAT criteria (p.74).

q: When communicating surface threat information to US Army or US Marine Corps rotary-wing aircraft and UAS, what format is used?
options: MGRS, with an attempt to provide at least a six-digit grid
explain: When communicating with US Army or US Marine Corps rotary-wing aircraft and UAS, pass surface threat information in MGRS, attempting to provide at least a six-digit grid (p.75).

q: If a status of a FADED GROUP is requested within 1 minute of the fade and the contact was NOT continuously tracked by a fighter, how should controllers respond?
options: With the last fighter location in a "FADED...LAST KNOWN" call
By anchoring the group at its last BULLSEYE position
explain: Unless the contact was continuously tracked by a fighter, controllers respond with "CLEAN" and "[number of] CONTACTs FADED" (p.76).

q: In response to a PICTURE request regarding faded contacts, what options do controllers have for reporting the number of contacts FADED?
options: An exact count up to ten only
explain: In response to a PICTURE request, controllers report "1," "2," or "multiple" contacts FADED, as appropriate (p.77).

q: After a controller voices a GROUP as FADED, what responsibility do the fighters retain for that GROUP if they can maintain correlation?
explain: Fighters will maintain MONITOR responsibility for a GROUP voiced FADED if they can maintain correlation (p.78).

q: When making a LEAKER call, what call sign should the entity use?
explain: The entity making the LEAKER call should use its own call sign (p.79).

q: In CMD PICTURE communication, which piece of information will always be included as core information?
options: The track number of the lead COVEY
explain: Inter-covey formation will always be included as core information in CMD PICTURE (p.80).

q: When using LEADING EDGE communication in CMD PICTURE, what must the controller communicate before communicating the LEADING EDGE itself?
explain: The controller should communicate the total number of COVEYs prior to communicating the LEADING EDGE (p.81).

q: According to the A/S serialization labeling convention, what does the designator "AA01" represent?
options: The first aircraft of package A
The first task of ATO day AA
explain: The labeling convention uses the ATO day and two-digit numeric sequencing, so AA01 is the first task of ATO day AA (p.83).

q: In the DLER attack briefing format, which information belongs in the "Remarks/Restrictions" field?
options: Deconfliction measures, ordnance, final attack heading, and TOT windows
The grid location compatible with the receiving system
explain: The DLER Remarks/Restrictions field includes deconfliction measures, ordnance, final attack heading, and TOT windows as required (p.84).

q: The read-back formats are listed from least restrictive/least radio time to most restrictive/most radio time. Which is the least restrictive option?
options: Voice response with task type and location
Voice response with task type, coordinates, elevation, and remarks
explain: The least restrictive, least radio time read-back is a WILCO digital response to J12.0 (p.85).

q: In ground threat system status terminology, what does ACTIVE mean?
options: Land or surface activity detected via communications intelligence
The emitter is radiating, derived from electronic intelligence
Ground equipment is at the tasked target location
A threat emitter derived from an off-board source
explain: ACTIVE means the emitter is radiating, derived from electronic intelligence (p.86).

q: In the TACREP alphanumeric designator table, what type of intelligence does the designator "E-##" indicate?
explain: Per the TACREP designator table, E-## indicates Electromagnetic intelligence (p.87).

q: When is TRESPASS voiced?
options: When a friendly aircraft loses sensor data
When an aircraft is within a not-previously-known SAM MEZ or any non-SEAD aircraft crosses inside the max recommended intercept range of a known SAM
When a group fades for more than 1 minute
When a cruise missile is positively identified
explain: TRESPASS is voiced when an aircraft is within a not-previously-known SAM MEZ (e.g., a POP-UP SAM) or any non-SEAD aircraft crosses inside the maximum recommended intercept range of a known SAM (p.88).

q: What is the minimum accuracy for a threat reaction transmission to be considered "high fidelity"?
options: A 5 nm semi-major ellipse, or as mission planned
explain: Minimum accuracy to consider a threat high fidelity is a 5 nm semi-major ellipse, or as mission planned (p.89).

q: In the F2T2EA chain, who is responsible for holding or delegating authority for aircraft or element search contracts under Find?
explain: Under Find, the MC is responsible to hold or delegate authority for aircraft or element search contracts (p.90).

q: What should a controller report if they do not hold the J12.6 once a target is called TARGETABLE?
explain: Controllers should report "HOLLOW [BULLSEYE]" if they do not hold the J12.6 (p.91).

q: What response do assets give if they have not found the object?
explain: Assets can respond with NO JOY if they have not found the object (p.92).

q: Under the Assess contract, what does SUCCESSFUL indicate?
options: Weapon impact was noted but effects were not generated
explain: SUCCESSFUL means desired weapon effects were generated and can be used to complete the F2T2EA chain (p.93).

q: Which two examples are given of fire support coordination measures?
options: High-density airspace control zones and transit levels
Coordinating altitude and restricted operations zones
Low-level transit routes and minimum-risk routes
explain: Fire support coordination measures include restrictive fire and no-fire areas (p.1).

q: According to the ACC publication, which Service has no TAC C2 capable entities?
explain: Among the TAC C2 capable entities by Service, the Army is listed as None (p.2).

q: During peacetime operations, the surface warfare commander and antisubmarine warfare commander are often combined into which single warfare commander?
options: The air and missile warfare commander
explain: During peacetime operations, the surface warfare commander and antisubmarine warfare commanders are often combined into a single warfare commander, the sea combat commander (p.3).

q: According to the Communication Priorities table, what is the number one priority for air communication?
explain: Table 1 lists flight safety and aircraft emergencies as priority 1 for air communication (p.5).

q: What is the purpose of the check-in procedure?
options: To assign new mission numbers to flights after launch
To establish contact, allow the airspace element to establish accountability of the airborne asset, and pass critical information before handoff to a final mission controller
To deconflict ground fires from aircraft transit routes
To terminate radar coverage of returning forces
explain: The purpose of check-in is to establish contact, allow the airspace element to establish accountability of the airborne asset, and pass critical information before handoff to a final mission controller (p.6).

q: When checking in with the final controller, what assumption is made about the on-coming aircraft?
options: The aircraft is assumed to be JACKAL SOUR
The aircraft is assumed to require a full position update
The aircraft is assumed to be TACADMIN complete
The aircraft is assumed to need a MICKEY
explain: When checking in with the final controller, the on-coming aircraft is assumed to be TACADMIN complete (p.7).

q: Per Table 2, what is the preferred primary digital means for verifying WORDS were received?
explain: Check-in procedures call for verifying WORDS received via digital means (e.g., primary J28.2) or voice (p.8).

q: Which of the following deviations should always be communicated by aircrew and controllers at check-in?
options: A change in the number of aircraft, such as a three-ship when four-ships were anticipated
explain: Deviations that should always be communicated at check-in include the number of aircraft (e.g., a three-ship flight when four-ships are anticipated) (p.9).

q: How should unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) check in with TAC C2?
options: By relaying through a manned flight lead
Via tactical chat using the same format as other aircraft
explain: Unmanned aircraft systems should check in with TAC C2 via tactical chat using the same format (p.10).

q: Per the J3.5 Track Identification Plan, a Downed Aircraft event is represented as which track type?
options: Friendly troop concentration search and rescue
J3.1 downed aircraft (bailout or pilot in water)
explain: In Table 4, a Downed Aircraft is represented as 'J3.1 downed aircraft (bailout or pilot in water)' (p.11).

q: How are WORDS designated and numbered?
options: By ATO day, numbered sequentially, and established and deleted individually
explain: WORDS are designated by ATO day, numbered sequentially, and established and deleted individually; e.g., WORDS AA01 is the first WORDS on ATO day 'AA' (p.12).

q: What is required for successful HAVE QUICK II/SATURN between communicators?
explain: Successful HAVE QUICK II/SATURN requires a synchronized time-of-day (TOD) signal, MICKEY, between all communicators (p.13).

q: For a voice fighter INFLTREP, how will controllers respond as a read back?
explain: For voice, controllers will respond 'COPY (CALLSIGN MISSION NUMBER), LINE 3 DETAILS' (p.14).

q: Which publication provides the example of a non-fighter INFLTREP referenced in Table 7?
options: JP 3-01, Countering Air and Missile Threats
explain: JP 3-09.3, Close Air Support, provides an example of a non-fighter INFLTREP for reference, as depicted in Table 7 (p.15).

q: During the Return to Base/Returning Forces phase, what should the TAC C2 check-in controller verify prior to friendly missile engagement zone (MEZ) entry?
options: That aircraft have passed a roll call
That aircraft are SQUAWKING appropriate modes and codes
That aircraft have received the LOWDOWN
That aircraft have completed a fidelity check
explain: TAC C2 should verify aircraft are SQUAWKING appropriate modes and codes prior to friendly missile engagement zone (MEZ) entry (p.16).

q: What are considerations for finding the bottom altitude of a stack when using procedural control?
options: Fuel state, ordnance, and abort code
Call sign, mission number, and PLAYTIME
explain: Weather, threats, and terrain are considerations for finding the bottom altitude of a stack (p.17).

q: What are the two techniques given for ensuring the required minimum forces are ready?
options: An INFLTREP or a WORDS update
A fidelity check or an ALPHA CHECK
A TAC C2-provided update or a roll call
explain: Two techniques for force package accountability are a TAC C2-provided update or a roll call (p.19).

q: When communicating with US Army or US Marine Corps rotary-wing and UAS, how should surface threat information be passed, if known?
options: In MGRS, with at least a six-digit grid
explain: When communicating with US Army or US Marine Corps rotary-wing and UAS, pass surface threat information in MGRS, if known, attempting to provide at least a six-digit grid (p.20).

q: In a LOWDOWN, short-range air defense (SHORAD) or tactical SAMs are included if they have been ACTIVE or AWAKE within what timeframe?
options: At any time during the mission
explain: SHORAD or tactical SAMs ACTIVE or AWAKE within the last hour may be included in a LOWDOWN (p.21).

q: Original TOT is 1500Z. TAC C2 hears "HARDROCK, HOSS 1, SLIP TOT 9 MINUTES." What is the new TOT?
explain: SLIP is not additive, so SLIP TOT 9 MINUTES sets the new TOT to 1509Z (p.22).

q: Before pushing assets to the tactical frequency during a lane handover, what should TAC C2 do?
options: Issue a ROLEX to all flights
Pass big-picture information to the on-coming lane commander
explain: TAC C2 should pass big-picture information to the on-coming lane commander before pushing assets to the tactical frequency (p.23).

q: Unless otherwise briefed, how is a YELLOW fuel/weapons status defined?
options: A status sufficient to continue the mission indefinitely
A status requiring immediate return to base
A status approaching a level insufficient to continue execution of the mission
A status insufficient to continue execution of the mission
explain: YELLOW means the aircraft is at a weapon and/or fuel status approaching a level insufficient to continue mission execution (p.24).

q: If a controller cannot determine the number of CONTACTs in a GROUP but assesses three or more, what fill-in is used?
explain: If unable to determine the number of CONTACTs but assessed to be three or more, controllers will only use the fill-in HEAVY (p.25).

q: What is the primary method to communicate a GROUP's location?
explain: BULLSEYE is the primary method to communicate the GROUP location (p.26).

q: Which format will controllers use when providing a THREAT call to an aircraft?
explain: Controllers will use the BRAA format when providing a THREAT call to an aircraft (p.27).

q: When may a controller voice "LAST" after an altitude?
options: When the GROUP is within 5 nm of BULLSEYE
When two GROUPs share the same altitude
When sensors have lost altitude data on a track but the controller has reported or offboard data for a last known altitude, up to 30 seconds after report
explain: Controllers may voice "LAST" after the altitude when sensors have lost altitude data but a last known altitude is available, up to 30 seconds after report (p.28).

q: Which specific aspects must be accompanied by a cardinal or sub-cardinal direction?
explain: FLANK, BEAM, and DRAG are accompanied with a cardinal/sub-cardinal direction (e.g., DRAG EAST) (p.29).

q: As a fill-in, the term HIGH indicates a contact at what altitude?
explain: HIGH means the contact is greater than 40,000 feet mean sea level and can be used as a fill-in (p.30).

q: What does the fill-in VERY FAST indicate?
options: Target speed greater than 900 knots or 1.5 mach
Target speed greater than 1.5 mach only above 40,000 feet
Target speed of 660-900 knots or 1.1-1.5 mach
Target speed greater than 660 knots or 1.1 mach
explain: VERY FAST is a target speed greater than 900 knots or 1.5 mach (p.31).

q: When must controllers add the IMPACT time to FRIENDLY surface-to-air fires?
options: When fighters are in COLD operations
When operating in a joint engagement zone or fighter engagement zone
When a GROUP is declared HOSTILE
explain: Controllers will add the IMPACT time to any FRIENDLY surface-to-air fires when operating in a joint engagement zone or fighter engagement zone (p.32).

q: Which of the following will NOT be included in the PICTURE or total number of GROUPs?
options: FRIENDLY, NEUTRAL, STRANGERs, and nonparticipating GROUPs
explain: FRIENDLY, NEUTRAL, STRANGERs, and nonparticipating GROUPs are not included in the PICTURE or total number of GROUPs (p.33).

q: When should the SMA PICTURE be given?
options: After fighter check-in, prior to the initial picture
Only after a NEW PICTURE is called
explain: The SMA picture should be given after fighter check-in, prior to the initial picture (p.34).

q: If an SMA PICTURE is passed via non-secure communications, what should be used for identification?
explain: If the SMA PICTURE is passed via non-secure communications, applicable code words should be used; NATO designators are used over secure communications (p.35).

q: What is an anchor in the context of a PICTURE?
options: The lowest-priority GROUP in the picture
A BULLSEYE used to establish the PICTURE and aid in fighter targeting
A GEOREF used for surface fires
A BRAA reference to a friendly fighter
explain: An anchor is a BULLSEYE used to establish the PICTURE and aid in fighter targeting (p.36).

q: Which of the following is one of the most common amplifying remarks that follows the dimensions of a traditional PICTURE?
explain: OPENING/CLOSING, TRACK, WEIGHTED, and ECHELON are the most common amplifying remarks (p.37).

q: What is the correct order of PICTURE amplification?
explain: PICTURE amplification order is OPENING/CLOSING, WEIGHTED, ECHELON, then TRACK direction (p.38).

q: A PICTURE is considered WEIGHTED under what condition?
options: Only one BOGEY GROUP is in the operating area
One or more GROUPs are offset so the distances between GROUPs are not equidistant
All GROUPs are HOT along the fight axis
The depth between GROUPs is less than 5 nm
explain: The PICTURE is WEIGHTED if one or more GROUPs are offset, meaning the distances between GROUPs are not equidistant (p.39).

q: When applying ECHELON, how is the distance between GROUPs passed?
explain: The distance between GROUPs will be passed as SEPARATION (p.41).

q: In a PICTURE, when is track direction assumed without the controller voicing it?
options: When only a SINGLE GROUP is present
When GROUPs share any common track direction
When all GROUPs are HOT along the fight axis
explain: If all GROUPs in a PICTURE are HOT along the fight axis, track direction is assumed (p.44).

q: When is the label SINGLE GROUP used?
options: When three or more GROUPs are separated in AZIMUTH
When exactly two GROUPs are detected in RANGE
When GROUPs cannot fit a traditional label
When one known BOGEY/BANDIT/HOSTILE GROUP is detected in an operating area
explain: The label SINGLE GROUP is used only when one known BOGEY/BANDIT/HOSTILE GROUP is detected in an operating area, and all information is passed as core information (p.46).

q: Which label is used if depth is equal to or less than 5 nm or CLOSING inside of 5 nm?
explain: WALL is the label used if depth is equal to or less than 5 nm or CLOSING inside of 5 nm (p.47).

q: A WALL is defined as how many GROUPs in what relationship?
options: THREE GROUPs or more separated in AZIMUTH
Three GROUPs with two in AZIMUTH and one in RANGE
explain: A WALL is THREE GROUPs or more separated in AZIMUTH (p.48).

q: In a WALL with more than five GROUPs, which GROUP is always closest to the first anchored outrigger GROUP?
explain: The SECOND GROUP will always be closest to the first anchored outrigger GROUP (p.49).

q: In describing a CHAMPAGNE, which dimension term is used first?
options: WIDE, to describe the AZIMUTH dimension
DEEP, to describe the RANGE dimension
explain: For a CHAMPAGNE, the term WIDE is used first to describe the AZIMUTH dimension and DEEP is used second for the RANGE dimension (p.50).

q: In a VIC, how are the two GROUPs in RANGE named?
options: LEAD GROUPS with a cardinal direction descriptor
TRAIL GROUPS with a cardinal direction descriptor
explain: In a VIC, the GROUP closest to the fighters is the LEAD GROUP and the two GROUPs in RANGE are named the TRAIL GROUPS with a cardinal direction descriptor (p.51).

q: In a BOX, how are GROUPs voiced?
options: Always from nearest to farthest GROUP
Clockwise or counterclockwise, based on anchoring priorities of the lead groups
explain: In a BOX, GROUPs will be voiced clockwise or counterclockwise, based on anchoring priorities of the lead groups (p.52).

q: In a LADDER with more than three GROUPs, what are the GROUP names?
options: NORTH GROUP, MIDDLE GROUP, SOUTH GROUP
FIRST GROUP through LAST GROUP by cardinal direction
LEAD GROUP, MIDDLE GROUP, TRAIL GROUP, REAR GROUP
LEAD GROUP, SECOND GROUP, THIRD GROUP, TRAIL GROUP
explain: When there are more than three GROUPs in a LADDER, the GROUP names are LEAD GROUP, SECOND GROUP, THIRD GROUP, TRAIL GROUP (p.53).

q: Which of the following is a stated reason to apply LEADING-EDGE communication?
options: When only a SINGLE GROUP is detected
When all GROUPs in an operating area do not fit a traditional label
When two PACKAGES are in separate lanes
When all GROUPs are HOT along the fight axis
explain: LEADING-EDGE communication is applied when all GROUPs in an operating area do not fit a traditional label, to minimize radio transmission, and to facilitate rapid targeting of priority GROUPs (p.54).

q: If PACKAGE bounding limits are defined by a known geographic boundary, what may controllers use to describe the relationship between PACKAGES?
explain: If bounding limits are defined by a known geographic boundary, controllers may use GEOREF to describe the relationship between PACKAGES (p.56).

q: When is the term NEW PICTURE used as opposed to PICTURE?
explain: The term NEW PICTURE will be used when fighters are HOT, while fighters executing COLD operations will request a PICTURE (p.57).

q: What drives the shift in communication priorities between controllers and fighters during an intercept?
options: The seniority of the flight lead
Which asset has the most SA to communicate time-sensitive information
explain: Priorities shift based on what asset has the most SA to communicate time-sensitive information (p.59).

q: Which is listed as a COMMIT criteria consideration during mission planning?
options: Weapon employment based (e.g., employ at first available employment range)
Cloud ceiling over the target area
explain: COMMIT criteria considerations include range based, location based, threat based, and weapon employment based, such as allowing fighters to employ at their first available employment range (p.60).

q: What does a fighter indicate by calling TARGETED on a specified GROUP?
options: That the fighter is maintaining awareness of and responsibility for the specified group
That the fighter has fired on the group
That the fighter has merged with the group
That the group has been declared HOSTILE
explain: A fighter that has called TARGETED indicates that fighter is maintaining awareness of and responsibility for the specified group (p.61).

q: When does a controller provide a QC TARGETING response?
options: After every fighter targeting call regardless of correlation
Only when the fighter requests confirmation
When fighter targeted communication does not correlate to a known group, match briefed shot doctrine, or to correct fighter misperception
Only when a data link dropout occurs
explain: Controllers respond with QC comparative communication when fighter targeted communication does not correlate to a known group, match briefed shot doctrine, or to correct misperception; if all information correlates, no response is provided (p.62).

q: After the tactical picture has been labeled, what may a DECLARE request use instead of the BULLSEYE location?
explain: When a declaration request is made after the tactical picture has been labeled, the GROUP name can be used instead of the BULLSEYE location (p.63).

q: Which is one of the three components to communicating each GROUP maneuver?
explain: The three components to each maneuver are recognizing the maneuver, communicating the relationship of the maneuver, and updating GROUP names/altitude (p.64).

q: Which call communicates that no CONTACTs are remaining HOT after a maneuver?
explain: Communicate MANEUVER AZIMUTH if all CONTACTs BEAM/DRAG with no CONTACTs remaining FLANK/HOT, indicating no CONTACTs remaining HOT (p.65).

q: If maneuvers occur inside the targeting range, which option is available to the controller?
options: Use UNTARGETED for the highest factor GROUP, ARM, or CONTACT not being TARGETED
Always issue a full NEW PICTURE
explain: Inside the targeting range, controllers can update each CONTACT with a new name, use UNTARGETED for the highest factor GROUP/ARM/CONTACT not being TARGETED, issue targeting per the game plan, or provide an abbreviated NEW PICTURE (p.68).

q: Two GROUPs with an AZIMUTH relationship maneuver to opposite sides in an AZIMUTH relationship. Which call applies?
explain: CROSSING is two GROUPs with an AZIMUTH relationship maneuvering to opposite sides in an AZIMUTH relationship, and is an informative call using the original PICTURE GROUP names (p.69).

q: What is a BOGEY DOPE request?
options: A request for information relative to ownship position on an indicated or closest GROUP to build/enhance fighter SA
A call indicating no sensor information on a GROUP
A directive call to commit on a GROUP
An interrogative call to declare a GROUP
explain: BOGEY DOPE is a request for information relative to ownship position on an indicated GROUP, or closest GROUP if not indicated, to build or enhance fighter SA (p.70).

q: How will a controller respond to a STROBE call?
options: By anchoring the GROUP off BULLSEYE
With range, altitude, aspect (HOT assumed), declaration, and GROUP name (if applicable) to the closest GROUP along that line of bearing
With BULLSEYE, altitude, and track direction
explain: Controllers respond to STROBE calls with range, altitude, aspect with HOT assumed, declaration, and GROUP name if applicable to the closest GROUP along that line of bearing (p.71).

q: Does a METALLICA call relieve a fighter of targeting responsibility for a GROUP?
options: Yes, it transfers responsibility to the controller
Only if the controller acknowledges it
No, METALLICA does not alleviate fighter targeting responsibility for a GROUP
explain: METALLICA does not alleviate fighter targeting responsibility for a GROUP, and controllers are not required to respond to it (p.72).

q: What should ANCHORED or STANDBY calls include if the GROUP does not have a name?
explain: ANCHORED or STANDBY calls should include the BULLSEYE location if the GROUP does not have a name (p.73).

q: No later than when is the THREAT call completed?
explain: The THREAT call is completed no later than threat range (p.74).

q: What term may fighters use to warn other flights or elements when TARGETED GROUPs maneuver and appear to meet THREAT criteria to them?
explain: Fighters may use the term HEADS UP to other flights or elements when TARGETED GROUPs maneuver and appear to meet THREAT criteria to the other flight or element (p.75).

q: Which set of brevity terms can a friendly element use to indicate a COLD flow in relation to the enemy?
options: EXIT, OUT, ABORT, COLD, or FLOW
explain: A COLD flow is indicated via EXIT, OUT, ABORT, COLD, or FLOW (p.76).

q: Why should controllers not anchor a group previously called FADED if the elapsed time since the fade exceeds 1 minute?
options: Due to system limitations and degradations in sensor SA
Because the BULLSEYE reference becomes invalid
Because the group is automatically reclassified as VANISHED
Because friendly fighters assume MONITOR responsibility
explain: Controllers should not anchor a group FADED more than 1 minute prior due to system limitations and degradations in sensor SA (p.77).

q: In training, when GROUPs are called DEAD by an RTO or a GROUP acknowledges a KILL call, what term do controllers use in response to DECLARE/STATUS requests to prevent erroneous targeting?
explain: In training, controllers use VANISHED in response to DECLARE/STATUS requests for GROUPs called DEAD or acknowledging a KILL, to prevent erroneous targeting (p.78).

q: How does a LANE CROSSER differ from a LANE RIDER?
options: A LANE CROSSER stays on the dividing line
A LANE CROSSER maneuvers into different areas of targeting responsibility
A LANE CROSSER is a positively identified cruise missile
A LANE CROSSER is always targeted by integrated fires
explain: A LANE CROSSER is a GROUP that maneuvers into different areas of targeting responsibility (p.79).

q: In CMD PICTURE communication for positively identified cruise missiles, the terms "GROUP" and "CONTACT" are replaced with which terms, respectively?
explain: When referring to positively identified cruise missiles, "GROUP" and "CONTACT" are replaced with "COVEY" and "QUAIL," respectively (p.80).

q: During CLOSE CONTROL, when does C2 transition to tactical control?
options: At the TARGETED call or at the GO TACTICAL call
After the initial vectors are provided
When the COVEY is within 35 nm
explain: C2 transitions to tactical control at the TARGETED call or at the GO TACTICAL call (p.81).

q: What does serialization imply about a task's order of creation?
options: It does not imply priority or confidence
It implies the controller's confidence level
explain: Serialization is applied to each task in the order of creation and does not imply priority or confidence (p.83).

q: In full digital tasking, which J-series message is the assumed data source unless the controller specifies another?
explain: J28.2 is the assumed data source unless the controller specifies another (p.84).

q: Who is responsible for maintaining the accuracy of the common tactical ground picture based on emitter or ROTATOR information?
explain: The SIGINT ID authority is responsible for maintaining accuracy of the common tactical ground picture based on emitter or ROTATOR information and resolving emitter ID ambiguities (p.85).

q: If a tasked asset is HOLLOW for the J3.3 or J3.5 message, what should be passed?
explain: If a tasked asset is HOLLOW for the J3.3 or J3.5 message, pass full voice tasking (p.86).

q: According to the TACREP alphanumeric designator table, what does the designator "S-##" indicate?
explain: Per the TACREP designator table, S-## indicates Survivor/isolated personnel (p.87).

q: When voicing TRESPASS, what heading does the voicing entity direct relative to the SAM?
options: A SNAP heading 90 degrees from the SAM
A SNAP heading 180 degrees away from the SAM
A SNAP heading to a HOT aspect on the SAM
A SNAP heading directly toward the SAM
explain: The voicing entity directs a SNAP heading 180 degrees away from the SAM and voices the SAM's bullseye, being directive then descriptive (p.88).

q: Which elements make up the Low-Fidelity threat reaction format?
options: Call sign, RWR indication, S/A threat BULLSEYE/LOCATION, DEFENDING with cardinal direction
Call sign, RWR indication, bearing, DEFENDING with cardinal direction, ownship BULLSEYE
Call sign, BRAA, aspect, declaration, fill-ins
Call sign, track number, TLE, DEFENDING
explain: The Low-Fidelity format is call sign, RWR indication, bearing, DEFENDING with cardinal direction, and ownship BULLSEYE (p.89).

q: Under the Fix contract, what fix quality is described as "inside 1 nm"?
explain: Low Accuracy is defined as inside 1 nm (p.90).

q: Which responses can an asset give if it is able to TRACK?
options: OCCUPIED, TALLY, TRACKING, CAPTURED, or MONITORING
HARM INBOUND, MAGNUM, EMPTY, or ARIZONA
explain: Assets can respond with OCCUPIED, TALLY, TRACKING, CAPTURED, or MONITORING if able to TRACK (p.91).

q: When an ATTACK plan is communicated via Link 16, what does each player need only to acknowledge?
explain: The plan can be communicated via Link 16, and each player needs only to acknowledge GOOD DATA (p.92).

q: If unable to find the target or object during the Assess SCAN, which terms may be relayed?
explain: If unable to find the target or object, relay VACANT, EMPTY, or NO JOY (p.93).

q: Which method of airspace control relies on positive identification, tracking, and direction of aircraft conducted with electronic means by an agency having authority?
explain: Positive control relies on positive identification, tracking, and direction of aircraft within an airspace, conducted with electronic means by an agency having the authority and responsibility therein (p.1).

q: Under whose tactical control do TAC C2 airspace control elements operate?
options: The JFACC, joint force maritime component commander, or composite warfare commander
Only the area air defense commander
The joint force land component commander exclusively
explain: TAC C2 are airspace control elements under tactical control of the JFACC, joint force maritime component commander, or composite warfare commander (CWC) (p.2).

q: The ASOC, DASC, or ACIT serves as the airspace control element for airspace control within what areas?
options: The fire support coordination line forward
All airspace above the coordinating altitude
explain: The ASOC, DASC, or ACIT is the airspace control element for airspace control within their assigned BMAs (p.3).

q: What is identified as the exception to the rule that TACADMIN does not cover operations outside the managed JOA or BMA?
options: An aircraft operating under military tower control in the terminal area
An aircraft departing an operating base under local air traffic control
An aircraft navigating routinely in civil airspace
An aircraft launched under scramble orders but operating in civil airspace
explain: An example of an exception is an aircraft launched under scramble orders (e.g., an alert status) but operating in civil airspace (p.5).

q: When relaying a call, what must be done with the relaying entity's call sign?
options: It is not included in the transmission
It is appended at the end of the message
It is stated first, before the recipient
It replaces the call sign of the entity being relayed
explain: When relaying a call, the format is '[entity speaking to], relay, [call sign of entity being relayed]' and the relaying entity's call sign is not included (p.6).

q: Per Table 2, what reference point is used to accomplish the ALPHA CHECK during check-in?
explain: Check-in procedures include 'Accomplish ALPHA CHECK from BULLSEYE' (p.8).

q: If aircrew are unable to make contact on the primary check-in NET, what procedures should they and TAC C2 refer to?
explain: If unable to make contact on the primary check-in NET, the aircrew and TAC C2 should refer to the CHATTERMARK procedures (p.10).

q: Per the J3.5 Track Identification Plan, how is a High-Value Individual event represented as a track type?
explain: In Table 4, a High-Value Individual is represented by the track type 'Hostile train' (p.11).

q: If correlation is in question for JACKAL SOUR fighters, what may they request prior to weapons employment?
options: The controller DECLARE a group, or BOGEY DOPE / BRAA to a GROUP
A WORDS update for the next ATO day
A new abort code and mission number
A roll call of the package
explain: If correlation is in question, fighters may request the controller DECLARE a group or request BOGEY DOPE, bearing, range, altitude, and aspect (BRAA) to a GROUP prior to weapons employment (p.12).

q: What is the first (highest priority) TOD source listed for HAVE QUICK II/SATURN?
explain: The prioritized list of TOD sources begins with Secure Global Positioning System (p.13).

q: When is TAC C2 NOT required to state "PROCEDURAL CONTROL IN EFFECT"?
options: When an aircraft first checks in
When the operating area standard is already procedural control
When moving from positive to procedural control
When the operating area standard is positive control
explain: If the operating area standard is procedural control, then TAC C2 does not need to state 'PROCEDURAL CONTROL IN EFFECT' (p.17).

q: If a flight does not respond to the roll call, what can the mission commander do?
explain: If a flight does not respond to the roll call, the MC can query TAC C2 (p.19).

q: An aircrew requesting LOWDOWN should allow how much time for TAC C2 to compile it?
explain: An aircrew may request LOWDOWN but should allow 30 to 60 seconds for TAC C2 to compile it, in accordance with communication priorities (p.20).

q: What does ROLEX adjust, and in what increments is it made?
options: Individual flight event timing in 1-minute increments
explain: ROLEX is used to adjust mission timing and is made in 5-minute increments and is not additive (p.21).

q: With whom does the criteria and authority for executing a CHATTERMARK reside?
options: The MC or a designated TL
explain: The authority for executing a CHATTERMARK resides with the MC or a designated TL (p.22).

q: During a lane handover sequence, what does the COMMIT call do?
options: Transitions communication priority to the committing aircraft
Allows on-coming aircraft to enter the operating area
explain: When directed by the lane commander, the COMMIT call transitions communication priority to the committing aircraft (p.23).

q: At which status must aircraft relay their state to TAC C2?
explain: Aircraft must relay when they are YELLOW to TAC C2 (p.24).

q: What is the assumed STRENGTH for all GROUPs unless otherwise specified?
explain: SINGLE CONTACT is the assumed STRENGTH for all GROUPs; more CONTACTs are specified (p.25).

q: If an aircraft or GROUP is within 5 nm of BULLSEYE, how may it be described?
explain: If an aircraft is within 5 nm of BULLSEYE, the GROUP may be described as AT BULLSEYE (p.26).

q: Why will BULLSEYE never be truncated to "bull"?
options: To avoid it being misinterpreted as "BRAA"
Because it conflicts with GEOREF code names
explain: BULLSEYE will not be truncated to "bull" to avoid it being misinterpreted as "BRAA" (p.27).

q: A controller can determine altitude but does not have a solution available. What does the controller voice?
explain: Controllers will use "ALTITUDE UNKNOWN" if the controlling platform is capable of determining altitude but a solution is not available (p.28).

q: When a GROUP is communicated via BULLSEYE, how is track direction expressed?
options: With the cardinal or sub-cardinal direction
With a specific aspect such as HOT or FLANK
explain: Track direction is communicated with the cardinal or sub-cardinal direction (e.g., TRACK SOUTH, TRACK NORTHEAST) when using BULLSEYE (p.29).

q: A GROUP is labeled HEAVY when it is known to contain how many CONTACTs?
explain: A GROUP is HEAVY if it is known to contain three or more CONTACTs (p.30).

q: When are LEANING ON assessments generally reserved for use?
options: When fighters are in COLD operations
explain: LEANING ON assessments are generally reserved for when fighters are in COLD operations (p.31).

q: When should controllers and fighters name an inner GROUP formation?
options: Only when the call will enhance fighter targeting or SA approaching the merge
On every PICTURE call regardless of range
Only when a GROUP is declared FRIENDLY
explain: Controllers and fighters should only name the inner GROUP formation when it will enhance fighter targeting or SA approaching the merge (p.32).

q: How many ways are there to communicate the PICTURE?
explain: There are five ways to communicate the PICTURE: core information, traditional labels, LEADING EDGE communication, WAVEs, and PACKAGEs (p.33).

q: If SMA positioning does not match a traditional label, how may the SMA be named?
explain: If SMA positioning does not match a traditional label, the SMA can be numbered (e.g., 1st SMA, 2nd SMA, 3rd SMA) (p.34).

q: With multiple GROUPs in azimuth, and unless utilizing directive targeting, which GROUPs are anchored?
explain: With multiple GROUPs in azimuth, unless utilizing directive targeting, only the outermost GROUPs are anchored (p.36).

q: If a traditional label does not apply based on the threat presentation, what communication should controllers use?
explain: If a traditional label does not apply based on the threat presentation, controllers should use LEADING EDGE communication (p.37).

q: What does the amplifying remark WEIGHTED describe?
options: Any GROUP that is HOT along the fight axis
A SINGLE GROUP at high altitude
Traditional labels with THREE GROUPs or more
explain: WEIGHTED is an amplifying remark that describes traditional labels with THREE GROUPs or more (p.39).

q: Using the 45-degree ECHELON line drawn from the GROUP closest to the fighters, where must the offset GROUP be for the PICTURE to be a RANGE?
explain: If the offset GROUP is aft of the 45-degree line, the PICTURE is a RANGE; if forward of it, the PICTURE is an AZIMUTH (p.41).

q: For a SINGLE GROUP label, how is the GROUP's information passed?
explain: For SINGLE GROUP, the label is SINGLE GROUP and all information is passed as core information (p.46).

q: What depth value do controllers use to determine the tactical relationship between GROUPs?
explain: Controllers use 5 nm of depth to determine the tactical relationship between GROUPs (p.47).
